subjects.subject: film-photography
subjects.title: The Exposure Triangle for Film
subjects.lesson:
First principles
Exposure is the total light that reaches your film. Three controls shape it: shutter speed (how long light hits the film), aperture (how wide the lens opening is), and ISO (how sensitive the film is). With digital you can freely change ISO. With film, ISO is baked into the roll you loaded. That makes shutter and aperture your main dials during a roll, and ISO your constraint.

Stops and balance
A stop is a unit of light. One stop more means doubling light; one stop less means halving. Shutter speed changes by stops (1/1000, 1/500, 1/250, 1/125, …). Aperture changes by stops too (f/1.4, f/2, f/2.8, f/4, f/5.6, f/8, f/11, f/16, f/22). To keep exposure steady, adding a stop in one place means removing a stop elsewhere. With fixed ISO film, you trade shutter for aperture to land on the same exposure.

What each control does to the look
- Shutter and motion: Fast shutters (1/500 and faster) freeze action. Slow shutters (1/30 and slower) blur moving subjects and camera shake. Handheld, many photographers start to risk blur below about 1/60 unless using a wider lens or careful technique.
- Aperture and depth of field: Wide apertures (f/1.4–f/2.8) isolate subjects with a blurred background. Narrow apertures (f/8–f/16) keep more of the scene in focus. Lenses tend to be sharpest a couple of stops down from wide open, often around f/5.6–f/8.
- ISO and grain: Higher ISO film (800, 1600) is more sensitive and allows faster shutters or smaller apertures in low light, but shows more visible grain and less fine detail. Lower ISO (100, 200) is smoother but needs more light.

Trade-offs that matter on film
Because ISO is fixed for the roll, you plan around it. With ISO 400, it is easier to freeze motion but harder to shoot wide open in bright sun without exceeding max shutter speed. With ISO 100, you get finer grain and more forgiving use of wide apertures outdoors, but low light becomes tough without a tripod.

Sunny 16: your built-in light meter
Sunny 16 is a rule of thumb for daylight. Set aperture to f/16 and shutter to the reciprocal of ISO for a front-lit subject in full sun. With ISO 100 film, that is 1/100 (use 1/125). With ISO 400, that is 1/400 (use 1/500). Adjust for conditions by opening the aperture in stops:
- Slight overcast: f/11
- Overcast: f/8
- Heavy overcast or open shade: f/5.6
- Deep shade or sunset: f/4
Bright scenes like snow or beach can be f/22. For backlit subjects, open 1 stop or reframe to meter the face.

How to make choices quickly
Start with the creative need. Do you need to freeze action? Pick the shutter first. Want background blur? Pick the aperture first. Then compensate with the other control to keep exposure. If both the shutter you want and the aperture you want are impossible in the light you have, that is an ISO mismatch: next time choose a different film speed, or use a tripod or flash.

Predicting effects when you change settings
- If you speed up the shutter by 2 stops (1/125 to 1/500) to freeze motion, open the aperture by 2 stops (f/8 to f/4) to keep exposure, accepting shallower depth of field.
- If you stop down the aperture by 3 stops for a landscape (f/4 to f/11) to gain depth, slow the shutter by 3 stops (1/500 to 1/60), accepting more risk of motion blur and maybe using a tripod.

Film latitude and safety margins
Color negative film is forgiving to overexposure by 1–2 stops, often producing rich color. It does not like underexposure; shadows get muddy. Slide film (E-6) is not forgiving and needs precise exposure. If in doubt with color negative, err slightly on the side of more light, as long as your shutter remains handholdable.

Metering with or without a meter
If you have a light meter, set it to your film ISO and meter the important tones, usually the subject’s face or midtones. If your meter dies or the scene is confusing, fall back to Sunny 16 as your baseline, then nudge based on subject tonality: open up for very dark subjects you want to see detail in; close down a bit for very bright subjects you want to retain texture.

Common pitfalls
- Forgetting ISO is fixed and chasing impossible combinations.
- Thinking f/16 means less light than f/11 because the number is bigger; remember f/16 lets in less light than f/11.
- Blurry images from too-slow shutter speeds while focusing on aperture alone. Use a minimum handholding speed roughly equal to 1 over the focal length (for a 50 mm lens, about 1/60).

A simple flow
1) Set ISO to film box speed. 2) Choose shutter or aperture based on creative need. 3) Use Sunny 16 or a meter for a starting exposure. 4) Trade stops between shutter and aperture to hit the same exposure. 5) Take a breath and watch edges, highlights, and motion.

Once this balance feels natural, you will spend less time worrying about numbers and more time seeing.
subjects.example: You are photographing your friend cycling past in bright sun with ISO 200 film. Sunny 16 suggests f/16 at 1/200 (use 1/250). To freeze motion, you want 1/1000, which is about 2 stops faster than 1/250. Open the aperture by 2 stops from f/16 to f/8. Final settings: 1/1000 at f/8. You keep exposure the same, freeze motion, and accept moderately deeper depth of field than f/4 would give.
subjects.scenario: Your in-camera meter suddenly dies during a hike. Midday sun, ISO 400 film. You set f/16 and 1/500 using Sunny 16. In a forest clearing under slight overcast, you open to f/11 and keep 1/500 for a portrait. Later, at a waterfall in deep shade, you open to f/4. To avoid blur at 1/500 you would be overexposed, so you slow to 1/30 at f/16 equivalent and use a rock as a tripod, accepting motion blur of the water while keeping the rocks sharp.
subjects.reflection_question: When your creative goal and the available light point you toward a setting that risks blur or lost depth, how do you decide which imperfection you are most willing to accept, and why?

subjects.subject: microeconomics
subjects.title: Incentives and Human Behavior
subjects.lesson:
First principles
People respond to incentives. An incentive changes the payoff of choices by altering benefits, costs, or social meaning. Behavior moves at the margin: small changes in incentives can shift which option becomes just a bit better than the alternatives.

Three families of incentives
- Monetary: cash, prices, wages, fines. Clear, countable, often fast acting. Example: a discount increases demand because the effective cost falls.
- Social: status, approval, reputation, belonging. People care what others think and how they rank. Example: a public leaderboard spurs extra effort without changing pay.
- Moral: identity, duty, fairness, values. People want to act in line with who they think they are. Example: voting or donating because it feels right, not for money.
These can reinforce or crowd out each other. A monetary incentive can undermine moral motivation if it reframes a good deed as a transaction.

Designing incentives
Good incentive design aligns what people want with what the system needs. Key levers are:
- Target: define the behavior, not just the outcome. Reward leading indicators (safe actions) as well as lagging results (no accidents) to avoid gaming.
- Salience and timing: make the incentive visible and timely. Immediate, certain rewards beat delayed, uncertain ones of the same size.
- Proportionality: size matters, but so does fairness. Too small feels insulting; too large tempts cheating.
- Framing: present it as gain or loss. People are often more sensitive to losses than equivalent gains.
- Simplicity: people must understand what earns what. Complex rules dilute motivation and invite loopholes.

Second-order effects
Every incentive has side effects. Ask, if this works, what happens next? If it fails, what gets worse? Classic examples:
- Daycare late fees: adding a small fine reframed lateness as a service to buy. Late pickups increased because parents felt they had paid for the extra time.
- Cobra effect: bounties on snakes led people to breed snakes to claim rewards. When the program ended, more snakes were released.
- Blood donations: paying can reduce supply by crowding out moral motives, lowering participation among those driven by altruism.
Anticipate gaming, shifting effort to measured metrics while neglecting unmeasured ones. Goodhart’s Law: when a measure becomes a target, it can stop being a good measure.

Predicting responses
Think about elasticity (how sensitive behavior is to incentive changes), constraints (time, skill, information), and identity (will this feel like who they are?). Consider nonparticipants: will the incentive attract people who otherwise would not join, and how might that change average quality or culture?

Practical process
1) Specify the behavior precisely. 2) Map stakeholders and what each values. 3) Choose a mix of monetary, social, and moral levers that point in the same direction. 4) Pilot small with measurement. 5) Watch for gaming and unintended shifts. 6) Iterate.

Common pitfalls
- Paying only for outcomes you do not fully control, which can punish good effort under bad luck, or reward luck over skill.
- Setting thresholds that create cliffs. People bunch just above the cutoff, and give up if too far below. Graduated rewards often work better.
- Ignoring fairness and voice. People reject incentives they view as illegitimate, even if financially attractive.

A simple mental model
Incentives are navigation buoys. Place them so the fastest route to personal goals goes through the channel you want. If you place them badly, people will still take the fastest route, even if it grounds the boat.

Measurement and feedback
Pair incentives with transparent feedback loops. Show people how close they are and what to do next. Recognize behavior publicly when social incentives help, and privately when public recognition would backfire.

Ethics
Design to elevate, not manipulate. Use incentives to remove friction from good choices and to make harmful options less attractive, while preserving dignity and autonomy.
subjects.example: A company pays a quarterly bonus for zero safety incidents. Reports drop, but minor injuries go unreported and risks accumulate. Redesign: keep a portion for outcomes, but add points for leading behaviors like near-miss reporting, hazard fixes completed, and team safety drills. Recognize teams publicly for transparent learning. Result: more reporting at first, then fewer incidents as hazards are surfaced and addressed.
subjects.scenario: A city plans congestion pricing to reduce downtown traffic. You forecast that peak-hour drivers with flexible schedules will shift to earlier or later times, freight will consolidate loads, and some commuters will switch to transit if service is reliable. Unintended effects could include traffic spilling into neighborhoods just outside the zone, and equity concerns for low-income drivers. Design tweaks: discounted off-peak rates, resident credits, reinvesting revenue in better transit, and monitoring spillover streets.
subjects.reflection_question: When you change what gets rewarded, which valuable behaviors that are hard to measure might shrink, and how can you protect them without blunting the incentive?

subjects.subject: Microsoft 365 Administrator (MS-102)
subjects.title: Navigating Microsoft 365 admin portals
subjects.lesson:
First principles
Microsoft 365 is a platform made of services: identity, mail, collaboration, security, compliance, and device management. The Microsoft 365 admin center (admin.microsoft.com) is the hub. From there you reach workload-specific admin centers for deeper tasks. Wayfinding starts with three anchors: tenant (the boundary of your org), roles (who can do what), and workloads (where a task truly lives).

Mental model: hub and spokes
Think of the main admin center as a map with blades. Blades are sections down the left navigation that group tasks. When a blade only scratches the surface, a link takes you to a dedicated portal. For example, you can create users in the hub, but advanced identity policies live in Microsoft Entra ID (entra.microsoft.com). Email basics appear in the hub, but transport rules live in the Exchange admin center (admin.exchange.microsoft.com).

Key blades in the Microsoft 365 admin center
- Home: service health and message center. Start here when users report issues. If Microsoft has an incident posted, troubleshooting shifts from local to waiting and communicating.
- Users: active users, guests, contacts. Create accounts, assign licenses, reset passwords. Quick tasks live here.
- Groups: Microsoft 365 groups, security groups, distribution lists. Create, manage membership, and naming policies.
- Roles: assign least-privilege admin roles. Global Admin is rare; prefer workload roles like Exchange Admin, Teams Admin, or Helpdesk.
- Billing: subscriptions, licenses, purchase services, and assign seats. Understand your available SKUs before promising features.
- Setup: guided tours and recommended configurations for common goals like email security or collaboration.
- Health: service health and usage reports. Check trends before you assume a feature is unused; maybe it is just new.
- Reports: adoption and product usage. Segment by department to target training.
- Org settings and integrated apps: global toggles for services and third-party integrations.

Workload-specific admin centers (where the deep controls live)
- Microsoft Entra admin center (entra.microsoft.com): identity and access. Conditional Access, multifactor auth settings, enterprise applications, roles, and Azure AD Connect health.
- Exchange admin center (admin.exchange.microsoft.com): mail flow, transport rules, connectors, shared mailboxes, retention for mail.
- SharePoint admin center (use the SharePoint Admin link from the hub): site management, sharing policies, storage, and OneDrive settings.
- Teams admin center (admin.teams.microsoft.com): meeting policies, messaging policies, voice, and Teams app permissions.
- Microsoft Intune admin center (endpoint.microsoft.com): device enrollment, compliance, configuration profiles, app deployment.
- Microsoft Defender portal (security.microsoft.com): security incidents, policies, attack surface reduction, email and collaboration protection.
- Microsoft Purview compliance portal (compliance.microsoft.com): data loss prevention, sensitivity labels, retention, audit, eDiscovery.

Efficient navigation habits
- Start broad, then narrow: begin in the hub to confirm health, license, and identity basics, then jump to the right workload.
- Use search: the top search in each admin center is fast. Search “transport rule” in Exchange, “Conditional Access” in Entra, or “DLP” in Purview.
- Pin favorites: in the hub, pin the blades you use daily. In workload portals, bookmark frequent pages.
- Keep profiles: separate browser profiles for test and production tenants to avoid cross-tenant mistakes.
- Learn the URLs: admin.microsoft.com, entra.microsoft.com, admin.exchange.microsoft.com, admin.teams.microsoft.com, endpoint.microsoft.com, security.microsoft.com, compliance.microsoft.com. Typing the right URL saves clicks.

Troubleshooting path
1) User reports a problem. 2) Check service health and message center in the hub. 3) Verify the user exists, is licensed, and is in the correct group. 4) Go to the workload portal that owns the feature. 5) Review configuration and policies. 6) Use diagnostic tools where available (Exchange mail flow trace, Teams call analytics, Intune device logs). 7) Record what changed last.

Least privilege and roles
Avoid using Global Admin daily. Assign workload-specific roles to technicians. In Entra, PIM (Privileged Identity Management) can make roles time-bound and approver-gated. In the hub’s Roles blade, review who holds high-privilege roles and why.

Communication
Use the Message center to anticipate changes that affect users. Summarize upcoming changes in plain language and link to training. This reduces tickets that are really about surprise, not failure.

Over time you will build a mental map: identity in Entra, mail in Exchange, content in SharePoint and OneDrive, chat and meetings in Teams, devices in Intune, protection in Defender, and data governance in Purview. The hub orients you; the workload portals let you act deeply.
subjects.example: A new employee starts tomorrow. In the Microsoft 365 admin center, go to Users > Active users > Add user. Enter name and username, set location, and assign a Microsoft 365 E3 license. After creation, click the account to set aliases and assign a Microsoft 365 group for the department. Then open admin.exchange.microsoft.com to create a shared mailbox permission if needed. If the user needs MFA, switch to entra.microsoft.com, create a Conditional Access policy targeting the user group, and enforce registration. You used the hub for identity and license basics, then workload portals for deeper email and access controls.
subjects.scenario: During a spike of login failures, you open admin.microsoft.com and see a service health incident for Microsoft Entra sign-in. You inform the helpdesk that it is a platform issue and set expectations. Still, one executive reports only Teams is broken. You confirm their license in Users, then open admin.teams.microsoft.com to check meeting policy assignments. Nothing unusual. You check security.microsoft.com for any user-specific blocks and entra.microsoft.com for Conditional Access sign-in logs. You find that a recent location condition is blocking the executive’s hotel IP. You add a temporary exception and document the policy adjustment.
subjects.reflection_question: When a user issue spans identity, mail, and collaboration, how do you decide which portal to open first, and what evidence would make you change direction quickly?

subjects.subject: wisdom
subjects.title: Reflection and Journaling
subjects.lesson:
First principles
Reflection closes the learning loop. You act, then you examine what happened, why it happened, and what to change. Without reflection, experience can be motion without insight. Journaling externalizes that reflection. It turns vague recollections into concrete notes your future self can use.

Why it works
- Attention: writing forces selection. Not everything fits on the page; you decide what mattered.
- Memory: retrieving an event and encoding it in your own words strengthens recall and builds patterns over time.
- Metacognition: you observe your own thinking, catch biases, and see the difference between stories you tell and facts you can verify.

A minimal daily routine (five minutes)
Pick a fixed anchor time: after dinner, on the train home, or before bed. Use one simple structure:
- What happened: neutral facts in a few lines. Names, decisions, outcomes.
- So what: meaning and feelings. What surprised you? What did you learn? Where were you off in your assumptions?
- Now what: one small next step or experiment. Tiny is fine; tomorrow’s nudge beats a grand plan you will not do.
Optional: add a tag or two for findability, like #meeting, #fitness, #decision.

Keep it frictionless
Use the tool you will actually use: a phone note, a paper notebook, or voice-to-text. Reduce startup friction by saving a template page with the three headings. Set a 3-minute timer; it prevents overthinking and keeps the habit light.

Make it safe and honest
Write for your future self, not for an audience. Honesty makes the journal useful. If privacy is a worry, keep it local and locked, or use a paper notebook. If judgment creeps in, switch to describing sensory details and actions before evaluating motives.

Weekly review (fifteen minutes)
Once a week, skim the last entries. Ask three questions: what keeps recurring, what moved forward, and what is stuck? Highlight two insights and schedule one concrete change. This step converts insight into better systems.

Handling emotions
Reflection is not rumination. Rumination circles the same thought without resolution. If you feel stuck, constrain the entry: name the feeling, write one sentence about the trigger, one about what you can control, and one kind action for yourself or someone else. Then stop.

Anchoring experiments
Use your journal to run small experiments. Formulate a hypothesis like, If I start meetings with a single clarifying question, we will finish faster. Try it for a week, record outcomes, then decide to keep, tweak, or drop. This builds a personal playbook grounded in evidence rather than hunch.

Common pitfalls
- Treating journaling as a performance. Keep it messy; beauty is optional, truth is not.
- Writing only when things go wrong. Capture wins and neutral days; patterns hide in the ordinary.
- Letting the journal grow without an index. Tags or a monthly summary keep it navigable.

Prompts that unlock depth
- The moment I hesitated today was…
- Something I did that my future self will thank me for is…
- A belief I tested today was…
- A question I want to carry into tomorrow is…
Use a single prompt when you feel blank.

A closing line
Aim for short, honest, and regular. Five minutes of clear seeing, repeated, outperforms occasional marathons of self-critique. Over time, you will notice fewer avoidable mistakes, more calm in decisions, and a growing catalog of playbooks that are yours.
subjects.example: After a difficult client call, you open a fresh note and write: What happened: Client asked for a discount; I said no; they threatened to leave; I offered a phased rollout; they agreed to trial. So what: I felt tense and rushed. I assumed a discount was the only lever. I forgot to ask about their real constraint, which was cash flow. Now what: Add a question to my call checklist, What is the real constraint? Tag: #sales #checklist. The next day, that checklist prompt leads you to propose a milestone-based payment plan before price even comes up.
subjects.scenario: You lead a small team. Each afternoon, you reserve five minutes before shutting your laptop. You write three short lines under the headings What happened, So what, Now what. On Fridays, you skim the week, see that unclear goals keep slowing Tuesday standups, and decide to try a one-sentence goal per task next week. You paste that rule at the top of the team’s agenda and note to review the change in a week’s time.
subjects.reflection_question: What is one question you could add to your daily template that, if answered honestly for a month, would most change your decisions?

subjects.subject: fiction-writing
subjects.title: Character Basics: Goal, Stakes, and Conflict
subjects.lesson:
First principles
A story is a person who wants something and struggles to get it. That simple engine is powered by three parts: a clear goal, meaningful stakes, and real conflict. When these are sharp, scenes pull the reader forward because every choice either moves the character closer, pushes them back, or changes what the goal costs.

Goal: point the story
A good story goal is concrete, visible, and time-bound. Win the tournament, deliver the package by midnight, get the promotion before layoffs. Avoid vague wants like be happy; translate them into an action the character can take or fail to take. A specific external goal gives the plot direction. The internal goal, often unspoken, is what the character thinks they need emotionally, like respect, belonging, or forgiveness. External goals structure action; internal goals shape meaning.

Stakes: why it matters
Stakes answer, so what if they fail? They must be personal, not abstract. If failure means losing a job, why is that devastating for this person? Maybe it risks a sibling’s medical care or confirms a lifelong fear of being invisible. Stakes also scale up as the story goes on. Early, the cost of failure might be embarrassment; later, it is identity, love, or safety. Stakes can be layered: external (the promotion), relational (their partner’s trust), and internal (self-respect).

Conflict: make it costly
Conflict is opposition that makes the goal hard. It can be external (antagonists, systems, nature) and internal (fear, flaws, false beliefs). The richest stories braid both: the world pushes back while the character’s own habits make it worse. Real conflict is about mutually exclusive aims. If both sides can peacefully get what they want without change, you do not have a story problem; you have a scheduling issue.

Try–fail cycles
Progress should meet resistance. The character tries, partially succeeds or fails, learns, and tries again. Each cycle raises the cost or tightens the deadline. This reveals character through choices under pressure. Readers bond not because the character is perfect, but because they keep choosing despite risk.

Designing your protagonist’s engine
- Name the external goal in one sentence. Include a deadline or constraint.
- Name the stakes in terms of loss that hurts and gain that matters.
- Name the main external obstacle and the internal habit or belief that trips them.
- Decide on a midpoint reversal where the goal becomes harder or the character realizes the real price.
- Plan how the internal and external lines collide in the climax, forcing a costly choice that reveals who they have become.

Story problem statement
Use a simple fill-in to pressure test: When inciting event happens, protagonist must pursue goal by deadline, or else stakes, but antagonist force and internal flaw stand in the way. If any part feels soft, the story will wander.

Scene alignment
Every scene should pose a question derived from the goal. Does this move them closer or farther? What new cost appears? If a scene could be removed and nothing changes, either tighten its goal or cut it.

Common pitfalls
- Goals that are secrets to the reader. Clarity fuels tension; readers can feel suspense even when they know the aim.
- Stakes that are global but not personal. Save-the-world stakes are hollow if we do not feel what it costs this character.
- Conflict that is solved by a conversation that could have happened earlier with no cost. Make the obstacle require growth or sacrifice, not convenience.

A note on change
Not all protagonists must change deeply, but the attempt to reach the goal should test their values. If they do not change, the world changes around them; if they do change, the climax should require them to act from the new self, paying a price the old self would not have paid.
subjects.example: A high school debater, Lena, wants a scholarship to escape a town where her family’s store is failing. External goal: win state by June. Stakes: without the scholarship, she cannot afford college, and her younger brother may have to quit sports to work. External conflict: a rival team that steals prep files. Internal conflict: Lena’s fear of being ordinary makes her hoard work and refuse help, burning out her partner. Try–fail cycles: an early tournament win breeds overconfidence; a midseason loss exposes her isolation; a coach offers a team-based strategy she resists. Midpoint reversal: the stolen files surface in a judge’s hands, threatening disqualification. Climax: Lena must choose between reporting the theft (risking a forfeit and the scholarship) or adapting the case on the fly and trusting her partner with the closing. She chooses trust, wins on originality, and the victory matters because it cost her the old habit of control.
subjects.scenario: You are pitching your novel to an agent in a loud coffee shop. You use the problem statement aloud: When a reclusive cartographer’s sister vanishes into a city’s abandoned subway, he must map the tunnels by the equinox to find her, or he loses the last person who believes in him, but a property developer wants the tunnels sealed and his panic disorder sabotages every descent. The agent now hears goal, stakes, and conflict in one breath, and you can field questions with a clear compass for scenes and revisions.
subjects.reflection_question: What hard choice at the climax would prove your protagonist has changed, and what price would make that choice feel earned rather than easy?
